core_concept: Investigating Forces: Motion, Friction, and Magnetism
facts: A force is a push or a pull that can start an object moving, stop it, or change its direction; without a force, a stationary object stays still.
Friction is a force that slows moving objects because their surfaces rub together; a ball rolls farther on a smooth floor than on rough carpet because the carpet has more friction.
Magnetism is a force that attracts only certain metals like iron and steel—not all metals; a magnet can pick up a paperclip but not an aluminum can because aluminum is not magnetic.
When two objects collide, the force from the collision can change their motion; a moving toy car hitting a stationary one makes the stationary car move forward while the moving car slows down.
You can predict motion by comparing forces: a harder push makes a toy car move faster and farther than a gentle push because stronger forces cause greater changes in motion.
misconceptions: Many students believe that a moving object must have a continuous force pushing it in the direction of motion, when in reality once it is moving it can keep going until friction or another force slows it down.
Students often think that magnets attract all metals, so they expect a magnet to stick to a copper penny or an aluminum foil, but only metals like iron and steel are attracted by magnets.
